title: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
relevant_articles: Article 13 - Abolition of laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights
summary: In this landmark case, the Supreme Court held that the principle that emerges from several earlier judgments is that, although Article 368 describes the procedure for amendment and the subject matter of amendments, the Constitution is nonetheless subjected to the limitations imposed on its amendment by the Constitution itself. The Constitution being a body of fundamental law, the courts' duty is to expound that fundamental law and defend it against infringement. The article 368 as it stands before us must be read as subject to these implied limitations. The Parliament cannot use the Article 368 of the Constitution to alter the basic structure of the Constitution. The basic framework cannot be destroyed. The Constitution is supreme law and no organ of the State can act in violation of it.
key_holdings: Parliament's amendment power under Article 368 is not unlimited
The Constitution has a basic structure that cannot be amended
Basic structure includes: sovereignty, federalism, democracy, secularism, and fundamental rights
Any amendment attempting to alter the basic structure is void
Courts have power to test constitutionality of amendments
key_quotes: The Constitution is the supreme law of the land and all State organs must function within its framework.
Although the Constitution is a body of fundamental law, it is nevertheless subject to limitations on its amendment imposed by itself.

The Constitution in its Preamble states that the people have resolved to give themselves a Constitution. Such a Constitution would be of a sovereign democratic republic. These ideas are part and parcel of the basic structure of the Constitution.

dissent.main_argument: The amending power is plenary and unrestricted; courts cannot impose implied limitations on Article 368
dissent.outcome: Minority view was rejected but influenced future jurisprudence

impact_and_significance: Limited the amending power of Parliament
Protected fundamental rights from being amended away
Foundation for numerous later constitutional judgments
Referenced in legal education across India
subsequent_cases: Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain (1975) - 42nd Amendment
Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980) - Reaffirmed basic structure
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994) - Applied basic structure
National Legal Services Authority v. Union of India (2014) - Basic structure and rights
amendments_related: 44th Amendment (1978) - Removed property rights from Part III
why_important: This case is fundamental to Indian constitutional law. It established that even constitutional amendments must respect certain basic principles. Without this doctrine, the government could theoretically amend the Constitution to eliminate democracy, fundamental rights, or federalism. This case protects the integrity of the Indian Constitution against majoritarian overreach.
bar_exam_relevance: Highly relevant for constitutional law in law school and bar exams. Candidates must know this landmark judgment.
accuracy_note: Information gathered from Indian Kanoon and verified against multiple legal sources
relevance_to_legal_finance_system: While primarily about constitutional law, this judgment demonstrates how courts protect fundamental rights including property rights. Relevant to understanding guardrails on government financial/tax regulations.
